title: 2D inverse heat conduction: recover conductivity k(x,y) from sparse sensors
governing_equations: \nabla\!\cdot(k(x,y)\nabla T)=q\ \text{on}\ (0,1)^2,\ T|_{\partial\Omega}=0;\ \text{recover}\ k\ \text{from sparse }T\text{ obs};\ k^*=1+\tfrac12\sin\pi x\sin\pi y
expected_band: recovered conductivity field k(x,y); relative-L2 vs k* ~1e-2-5e-2 (loosest where |grad T| is small, near the boundary)

variants.note_en: Conductivity field recovered from ~100 sparse noisy T sensors; relative-L2 vs the exact k*.
variants.note_es: Campo de conductividad recuperado desde ~100 sensores T dispersos y ruidosos; L2-relativo vs el k* exacto.

comparison.note_en: INVERSE problem: recover the conductivity k(x,y). PURE PHYSICS with no sensor data leaves k underdetermined - the recovered field is nowhere near k* (L2 = 356% vs the analytic standard). Add the ~100 sparse T sensors and physics+data recovers it (L2 = 4.0%). The DATA is what makes the inverse solvable.
comparison.note_es: Problema INVERSO: recuperar la conductividad k(x,y). La FISICA PURA sin datos de sensores deja k indeterminada: el campo recuperado no se parece a k* (L2 = 356% vs el estandar analitico). Con ~100 sensores T dispersos, fisica+datos lo recupera (L2 = 4.0%). Los DATOS son los que hacen soluble el inverso.

estimate.question_en: Where is this plate insulating vs conducting? (defect mapping from ~100 point temperatures)
estimate.question_es: ¿Dónde aísla y dónde conduce esta placa? (mapa de defectos desde ~100 temperaturas puntuales)
estimate.why_en: A FIELD unknown is a native PINN inverse: the map is just another network output fed by sparse data; classical inversion needs a mesh + adjoint + many forward solves.
estimate.why_es: Una incógnita de CAMPO es un inverso nativo de PINN: el mapa es otra salida de la red alimentada por datos dispersos; la inversión clásica exige malla + adjunto + muchas resoluciones directas.

governing_equations_es: \nabla\!\cdot(k(x,y)\nabla T)=q\ \text{en}\ (0,1)^2,\ T|_{\partial\Omega}=0;\ \text{recuperar}\ k\ \text{desde datos dispersos }T\text{ obs};\ k^*=1+\tfrac12\sin\pi x\sin\pi y
